includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom

items.title: Oil market never been this mispriced — US inventories hit tank bottom by late July
items.idea: 11M b/d of supply shut in from Hormuz closure. China cut imports ~3.5M b/d but only partially offsets. US commercial crude reaches operational minimums (~380M bbls) by late July regardless of whether Hormuz reopens — empty VLCCs need 6 more weeks to return to the Persian Gulf after any opening. 3 of the largest crude draws since 1982 already occurred in the last 6 weeks.
items.angle: The market is pricing a swift diplomatic resolution. The physics of tanker logistics says that even if Hormuz opened today, the supply relief is 6+ weeks away. There is no scenario where inventories don't hit critical levels by late July.
items.tweetA: 11M b/d shut in. China cut imports 3.5M b/d but that isn't enough. US commercial crude hits tank bottom by late July whether Hormuz opens or not — VLCCs still need 6 weeks to return after opening. The market is pricing a swift resolution. The logistics say otherwise. $USO $XLE
items.tweetB: The SPR bridge is running out. Three of the largest US crude draws since 1982 happened in the past 6 weeks. When government intervention slows at end of June, commercial inventories absorb the full Hormuz outage. This is not priced. $XLE $CL_F

items.title: Iran-US: binary scenario guarantees prolonged conflict — no middle ground
items.idea: HFI Research frames the Iran-US conflict as all-or-nothing: either the IRGC retains control of the Strait of Hormuz, or the regime is toppled. There is no moderate outcome. This binary structure makes swift resolution structurally impossible, contrary to what oil markets have priced for 90+ days.
items.angle: Pezeshkian's reported resignation and IRGC hardliners ascending removes the moderate counterparty. The oil premium should come back.
items.tweetA: Iran: IRGC controls Hormuz OR the regime falls. No middle ground. Markets have priced in swift resolution for 90+ days. IRGC hardliners now reportedly more in control after Pezeshkian's exit. The oil premium comes back. $XLE $USO $BNO
items.tweetB: The Iran conflict doesn't have a moderate outcome. Either IRGC wins and Hormuz stays closed, or the regime is toppled. Markets are pricing a ceasefire. They are not pricing regime change. Those are very different oil scenarios. $USO $XOM $CVX

items.title: Hormuz now 90+ days closed; SPR runway ends June — government tools exhausted
items.idea: Hormuz closure past 90 days. SPR release velocity slows materially by end of June. After that, commercial inventories absorb the full force of supply outage with no government buffer.
items.angle: The SPR release bought time. That time is running out. The inflection where intervention stops masking the physical shortage is weeks away.
items.tweetA: Hormuz: 90+ days closed. SPR releases slow by end of June. After that, government has no more tools. Commercial crude inventories absorb the full outage — no buffer. Watch June-July crude draws. $XLE $MPC $VLO
items.tweetB: The SPR release was always a bridge, not a solution. The bridge ends late June. On the other side is the full 11M b/d supply shock hitting commercial inventories. This inflection is not in oil futures pricing today.

items.title: MacroEdge upgrades to super bubble — final liquidity injections identifiable in real time
items.idea: MacroEdge upgraded from 'bubble' to 'super bubble.' Tells: pattern day trading rule eliminated, SpaceX pre-IPO investor minimum lowered. Classic final-stage liquidity injections — broadening the buyer pool when organic demand isn't enough.
items.angle: Every bubble has final-stage tells. The tells here are regulatory — removing friction for retail participation. These are not bullish signals. They are last-round-of-music signals.
items.tweetA: MacroEdge upgraded from bubble to super bubble. The tells: pattern day trading rule eliminated, SpaceX pre-IPO minimum lowered. Final liquidity injections — removing friction to pull in the last wave of retail capital. Healthy bull markets don't need these tricks. $QQQ
items.tweetB: When regulators remove the pattern day trading rule and lower SpaceX pre-IPO minimums in the same week, that is not confidence. That is a system that has run out of organic buyers. The question is not if this ends but what triggers the unwind.

items.title: Korea tops MacroEdge Global Bubble Index; Yen carry trade is hidden fuel for US rally
items.idea: Korea has the most severe reading in the MacroEdge Global Bubble Index. The Yen carry trade has been primary support for both Korean and US equities. If the Yen strengthens, Korea — the most extreme bubble — falls first.
items.angle: US indices feel stable because the carry trade is invisible in daily tape. Korea is where instability is becoming visible. Korea wobbling is the forward indicator.
items.tweetA: Korea ranks highest on the MacroEdge Global Bubble Index. The Yen carry trade is the hidden fuel behind both the Korean and US rallies. Korea wobbling is the canary. Watch $EWY as a leading indicator for $SPY.
items.tweetB: US markets supported by Yen carry that doesn't show up in headlines. Korea is the most extreme AI bubble globally and it's already wobbling. The canary is singing. The miners aren't listening.

items.title: May job cuts 82.7K — data center buildout masking cyclical weakness like shale 2011-2014
items.idea: MacroEdge Job Cuts Tracker: 82.7K cuts in May. Unemployment holds low because data center buildout is absorbing labor in construction — same dynamic as shale 2011-2014. When capex peaks, underlying weakness emerges rapidly.
items.angle: When shale capex peaked in 2014, labor market deterioration was sharp and fast. Data center is the new shale — geographically distributed but same capex-peak dynamic.
items.tweetA: May job cuts: 82.7K. Unemployment still holds low because data center construction absorbs displaced workers — exactly what shale did 2011-2014. When hyperscalers slow capex, the masked weakness reappears fast. $XLI $ITB
items.tweetB: 82.7K job cuts but nobody panics because data centers are hiring. I've seen this movie: shale absorbed everyone 2011-2014. When oil fell, those jobs vanished overnight. Data center is the new shale. The capex cycle is approaching its peak. Watch Q3.

items.title: TSMC CEO: chip supply won't meet AI demand for years — structural ceiling on the buildout
items.idea: TSMC CEO C.C. Wei at annual shareholder meeting: supply will fall short of AI-fueled demand for years. Won't fulfill demand from American customers even as US capacity comes online. Hyperscalers spending $725B on AI this year. TSMC holding 30%+ growth forecast; staff bonuses also up 30%+.
items.angle: Every AI capex model assuming demand can be met in a reasonable timeframe is wrong. The chip constraint is structural and multi-year.
items.tweetA: TSMC CEO: 'It will be a long time before we can meet customer demand.' $725B in hyperscaler AI spend this year. 30%+ revenue growth. Still can't meet demand. The chip constraint is structural. Every AI capex model that ignores this is wrong. $TSM
items.tweetB: TSMC: 30% revenue growth, staff bonuses up 30%, capex at top of $56B range — and still can't meet demand. The bottleneck isn't capital. It's the physical impossibility of building fabs fast enough. The AI buildout has a hard ceiling. $NVDA $AMD $TSM

items.title: TSMC won't hike prices — margin capture flows to Nvidia and hyperscalers, not the fab
items.idea: Unlike memory chips, TSMC explicitly restrains price increases despite severe supply scarcity. Effect: scarcity premium flows upstream to Nvidia (which does raise prices) and hyperscalers. TSMC absorbs the constraint; customers capture the economic rent.
items.angle: TSMC's restraint is Nvidia's pricing power. Understanding where the margin lands in a constrained system is key to understanding AI sector valuations.
items.tweetA: TSMC refuses to hike prices despite extreme supply scarcity. The margin goes upstream. Nvidia captures premium pricing. TSMC's restraint is Nvidia's pricing power. $NVDA is the beneficiary of TSMC's generosity. $TSM $NVDA
items.tweetB: Memory chips hiked prices in a shortage. TSMC explicitly won't. The scarcity rent flows up the stack to whoever has pricing power. That's Nvidia. Every dollar TSMC doesn't take in higher prices is a dollar Nvidia captures in GPU margins.

items.title: TSMC US fab: $265B total commitment — 10 fabs planned, decade of Arizona capacity
items.idea: As part of the US-Taiwan trade pact, TSMC plans at least 10 US chipmaking plants total (6 at $165B + 4 more at ~$100B additional). Two Arizona plots acquired sufficient for a decade. Elon Musk's aspirational Terafab with Intel adds another vector.
items.angle: $265B from TSMC alone is the largest single-company US manufacturing commitment in history. Construction supply chain implications (power, water, labor) are staggering.
items.tweetA: TSMC: 10 US fabs planned, $265B total commitment, Arizona plots handling a decade of capacity. Largest single-company US manufacturing commitment in history. The chip geopolitics trade is a decade-long infrastructure story. $TSM
items.tweetB: $265B in planned US fab investment from TSMC alone. The US semiconductor industrial policy is the largest peacetime manufacturing bet in history. Arizona's power, water, and labor markets are not ready. $TSM $AMAT $LRCX

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Anti-Goldilocks NFP: weak = stagflation, strong = yields up — options pricing cheapest straddle since 2024
items.idea: Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan flag today's NFP as anti-Goldilocks: weak print = stagflation concerns (Fed can't cut with PCE at 3.8%); strong print = yields higher. S&P straddle into this release is near its cheapest level for an NFP since late 2024 — market underpricing vol.
items.angle: Analysts warn of larger move; options market is complacent. When GS/JPMorgan and the options market disagree this sharply on event risk, one is wrong. History favors the analysts.
items.tweetA: Today's NFP is anti-Goldilocks: weak = stagflation fear (no Fed rescue). Strong = yields spike. Goldman and JPMorgan warn this report could rattle markets. The S&P straddle is at its cheapest for NFP since late 2024. The vol is underpriced.
items.tweetB: The options market is pricing very little drama for NFP. Goldman and JPMorgan are flagging elevated risk. The last time the straddle was this cheap for NFP was late 2024. One side of this trade is wrong. The complacency looks like the wrong side.

items.title: The Fed put is on holiday — bad news is bad news again; RSI at 99th percentile since 1991
items.idea: PCE at 3.8% means Fed cannot cut to rescue equities from weak data. Regime flipped: 2023 bad data = cut hope = rally. Now bad data = stagflation fear. RSI on equities ~78 — 99th percentile of risk appetite since 1991. Stocks at records going into a binary event with no policy backstop.
items.angle: Asymmetry is extreme. Goldilocks print is the only scenario where both bonds and equities benefit. The risk/reward for being long duration or high-beta tech into this print is terrible.
items.tweetA: Inflation 3.8%, RSI ~78 (99th percentile since 1991), stocks at records. The Fed put is gone. Bad news is bad news again — no rate cut rescue available. This isn't 2023. The setup into NFP is the asymmetrically worst it has been in three years. $SPY $QQQ
items.tweetB: The regime flipped and most participants haven't adjusted. 2023: bad data → cut hope → stocks rally. 2026: bad data → stagflation → stocks fall. RSI at 78 means the crowded position is long. That's who gets hurt first. $SPX

items.title: Warsh Day 13 — first FOMC June 16-17; trade: long banks/energy, short TLT/REITs
items.idea: Kevin Warsh 13 days into Fed Chair role. First FOMC June 16-17, 97% probability of hold. Trade: long XLF, JPM, GS, KRE, XLE, PGR, BRK.B, GLD vs short TLT, XLRE, ITB, QQQ, ARKK. Invalidation: 100-130K jobs with cooling wages and 4.3% unemployment.
items.angle: Warsh's first meeting is the first major test of his reaction function. Market has no price discovery on it. Uncertainty argues for long banks over duration risk.
items.tweetA: Warsh is 13 days in. First FOMC June 16-17. 97% probability of hold. Zero price discovery on his reaction function. Into that uncertainty: long banks (XLF, KRE), long energy (XLE), short long bonds (TLT) and homebuilders (ITB). $XLF $TLT
items.tweetB: The cleanest 2026 trade is a regime call not a market call. Higher-for-longer is now structural. Warsh won't rescue duration. Long BRK.B, JPM, XLE. Short TLT, XLRE, ARKK. The complication is everyone knows it and not everyone is positioned yet.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Samsung + SK Hynix = 75% of KOSPI gains — concentration risk at extreme
items.idea: Samsung and SK Hynix account for 50%+ of KOSPI index weight and ~50% daily turnover. Nearly 75% of KOSPI gains this year trace to those two companies. Foreign investors steadily reducing exposure. Won weakening. Korean bond yields rising.
items.angle: Concentrated rallies don't reverse gently. When the two stocks responsible for 75% of gains correct, the index falls 2-3x faster than it rose.
items.tweetA: Samsung + SK Hynix: 75% of KOSPI gains, 50%+ of index weight and daily turnover. This is not a bull market — it's a two-stock leveraged bet on AI dressed as an index. When those two correct, the KOSPI doesn't dip — it collapses. $EWY
items.tweetB: The KOSPI is not an index. It's Samsung and SK Hynix with filler. 75% of gains from two stocks. A rally that narrow is a concentration risk, not a market signal. The moment those names crack, the Korea AI boom becomes the Korea crash overnight.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Korea: margin balances at record highs, brokerage deposits falling — leverage replacing liquidity
items.idea: Korean brokerage deposits fallen sharply while margin balances surged to record highs. Healthy bull markets fueled by fresh cash. Korea: leverage replacing liquidity — investors borrowing more aggressively as cash cushion shrinks. Textbook late-cycle warning.
items.angle: Margin/deposit divergence is one of the most reliable late-cycle indicators. When it peaks, reversal is margin-call driven and therefore fast and non-linear.
items.tweetA: Korea: margin balances at record highs. Brokerage deposits: falling sharply. Leverage replacing liquidity is the textbook late-cycle signal. The bull market running on borrowed money with a shrinking cash cushion is a margin call waiting to happen. $EWY
items.tweetB: Fresh cash fuels healthy bull markets. Korea's is running on margin. Deposits down, margin at records. Every point of upside from here adds more leveraged longs that need unwinding in any correction. The cascade math gets exponentially worse.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Leveraged Samsung/SK Hynix ETFs create forced-selling feedback loop — amplifiers, not shock absorbers
items.idea: Leveraged ETFs tied to Samsung and SK Hynix enormously popular in Korea. Structure requires buying when markets rise and selling when they fall — feedback loop amplifying volatility. Not shock absorbers — accelerants. Vol when AI trade turns in Korea will be extraordinary.
items.angle: Most retail investors using leveraged ETFs believe they're getting 2x returns. They don't understand the rebalancing mechanism that forces systematic selling into falling markets.
items.tweetA: Koreans piled into leveraged Samsung and SK Hynix ETFs. These products buy on rallies and sell on dips automatically. Not shock absorbers — accelerants. When the AI trade turns in Korea, the forced selling amplifies the move beyond what fundamentals justify. $EWY
items.tweetB: Leveraged single-stock ETFs on Samsung/SK Hynix: massive AUM, poorly understood mechanics. The rebalancing that makes them work in trending markets is the same mechanism that makes corrections catastrophic. Korea has built a vol-amplification bomb into its most crowded trade.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Korean politicians suggest Samsung share excess AI profits — political risk enters crowded trade
items.idea: Korean policymakers suggested Samsung and others should share excess profits from the AI boom. Whether it becomes policy is beside the point — the uncertainty itself damages investor confidence in the most crowded trade in Asia, at exactly the wrong moment.
items.angle: Political risk on profit redistribution is the hardest to hedge. It doesn't show up in earnings models — it shows up in multiple compression.
items.tweetA: Korean politicians: Samsung should share excess AI profits. Policy or not, the conversation itself is the damage — it arrived into a market that was already leveraged, concentrated, and seeing foreign outflows. How do you model profit-sharing risk in your Samsung DCF? You don't. $005930
items.tweetB: Profit-redistribution rhetoric targeting Samsung at exactly the wrong moment: margins at record, leverage at record, foreign selling underway. Markets price the uncertainty before the legislation. Add political risk to concentration risk and margin risk. Korea's risk stack just got taller.

items.title: Israel-Lebanon OFFICIAL ceasefire — oil -4%, but Hormuz still closed and IRGC hardliners in charge
items.idea: Israel and Lebanon reached official ceasefire with US joint statement, sending WTI -4% to $92.18 and Brent -3.23% to $94.65. Market confused Lebanon with Hormuz: Strait remains closed (separate Iran conflict). Pezeshkian reportedly resigned, IRGC hardliners ascendant. 60-day Hormuz MOU still unsigned.
items.angle: The oil market sold Lebanon relief into a Hormuz problem. These are different conflicts. Lebanon ceasefire = tactical de-escalation. Iran/Hormuz = still binary, still unresolved, now with hardliner government. Premium should come back.
items.tweetA: Israel-Lebanon ceasefire: WTI -4% to $92.18. The market sold oil on Lebanon peace — but Hormuz is a different conflict. Strait still closed. Iran's president reportedly resigned. IRGC hardliners ascendant. The 60-day Hormuz MOU still unsigned. This oil selloff is a buying opportunity. $XLE $USO
items.tweetB: Oil traders sold Lebanon ceasefire as if it solved Hormuz. It doesn't. Two separate conflicts. Lebanon peace is real. Iran-Hormuz closure is also real. With Pezeshkian reportedly out and IRGC in charge, the Hormuz resolution just got harder. Reload energy longs.

items.title: Iran President Pezeshkian reportedly resigned — IRGC hardliners ascendant, deal thesis collapses
items.idea: Iran's President Pezeshkian reportedly resigned, IRGC hardliners now ascending. Entire ceasefire negotiation framework assumed Iran had moderate leadership willing to deal. That assumption is now gone. The IRGC controls Hormuz and has political incentives to maintain the closure.
items.angle: The most important geopolitical development this morning that markets have not fully priced. The deal-by-summer thesis required Pezeshkian. Without him, the Hormuz closure is IRGC-managed.
items.tweetA: Iran President Pezeshkian reportedly resigned. IRGC hardliners now in control. The ceasefire thesis assumed a moderate counterparty. That counterparty just left the building. The IRGC that controls Hormuz has no incentive to reopen it. Oil put this on your radar. $XLE $BNO
items.tweetB: The Hormuz deal thesis: Pezeshkian + US talks = reopening by summer. New reality: Pezeshkian reportedly out, IRGC in control. The faction that closes straits is running Iran. Markets haven't repriced this. The oil premium that sold off on Lebanon news comes back.

items.title: April FOMC: 8-4 vote to hold — most dissents since 1992; Polymarket 68.8% zero cuts in 2026
items.idea: April FOMC vote was 8-4 to hold at 3.50-3.75%, the most dissenting votes since 1992. Warsh inherits a fractured committee on Day 13. Polymarket prices 68.8% probability of ZERO Fed cuts in 2026.
items.angle: A 4-dissent Fed cannot communicate clearly or move decisively. That uncertainty alone is a risk premium markets haven't priced. Warsh's first meeting will either clarify or deepen it.
items.tweetA: April FOMC: 8-4 vote. Most dissenting Fed members since 1992. Warsh inherits a fractured committee. Polymarket: 68.8% chance of ZERO cuts in 2026. The Fed that was supposed to be the backstop is internally divided and frozen by PCE at 3.8%. This is the new normal. $TLT $GLD
items.tweetB: Four Fed members dissented in April — most since 1992. A committee this divided cannot give clear forward guidance. Warsh walks into a room where 4 of 12 colleagues disagreed with the last decision. That is not a cohesive central bank.

items.title: BROADCOM -13-15%: Hock Tan held $100B AI target, didn't raise — is AI capex peaking goes mainstream
items.idea: Broadcom fell 13-15% after Hock Tan kept $100B full-year AI chip target but declined to raise it. Markets had priced a raise. Revenue $22.19B missed $22.27B. HSBC simultaneously flagged chip-price slides and AI spending slowdown as its biggest worries. First time the question of AI capex peaking moved from fringe to mainstream.
items.angle: The AVGO beat on EPS but missed on revenue. The signal isn't the miss — it's the maintained guidance. Hock Tan chose not to raise. That choice is the most bearish data point for the AI capex cycle since it started.
items.tweetA: Broadcom -15% after Hock Tan held the $100B AI target and did NOT raise it. Markets priced a raise. That gap is the signal. When the CEO of the second-largest AI chip company won't raise guidance, the AI capex accelerating narrative is officially in question. $AVGO $NVDA $SMCI
items.tweetB: AVGO: EPS $2.44 beat $2.40. Revenue $22.19B missed $22.27B. CEO kept $100B, didn't raise. The number wasn't the story. The guidance restraint was. Hock Tan saw the same demand environment and chose not to commit to more. That tells you everything. $AVGO

items.title: BTC tests $61,500 — $1.72B liquidations, 13-day ETF outflow record $4.4B; IBIT alone -$3.3B
items.idea: Bitcoin plunged to test $61,500 (below pre-war levels). $1.72B total liquidations in 24h (longs $1.41B). Spot BTC ETFs set 13-day outflow record ~$4.4B total, BlackRock IBIT accounting for ~$3.3B. BTC down 20% since May 15. The sticky institutional ETF wrapper proved not sticky in a real drawdown.
items.angle: The ETF wrapper that drove the 2024 bull run is now the incremental seller. $3.3B out of IBIT means the same institutions that provided buy-side flow are now the redemption pressure.
items.tweetA: BTC tests $61,500. $1.72B liquidations in 24h. Spot ETFs: 13-day outflow record, $4.4B out — BlackRock IBIT alone $3.3B. The sticky institutional money that drove the 2024 rally is the incremental seller now. The ETF wrapper that helped it go up is now the exit door. $IBIT $GBTC
items.tweetB: BTC down 20% since May 15. $4.4B out of spot ETFs in 13 days. This is institutional redemption through the very product that was supposed to legitimize Bitcoin. When your biggest bull-market buyers become sellers, dip-buying needs more than hope. Wait for the flush.

items.title: MSTR broke 4-year rule, sold BTC for first time — Saylor says AI rotation but Broadcom just missed
items.idea: Strategy (MSTR) sold Bitcoin for the first time in four years under leveraged pressure, with cost basis $75,700 and BTC below that. Saylor attributed it to AI rotation. But Broadcom missed AI guidance and Nasdaq sold off same day. No clean rotation destination. Money is going to defensives.
items.angle: Saylor's AI rotation narrative doesn't survive Broadcom's miss. The real signal: largest corporate BTC holder broke a 4-year rule under pressure. Forced action dressed as a thesis.
items.tweetA: Strategy sold BTC for the first time in 4 years. Cost basis $75,700 — now underwater. Saylor calls it AI rotation. But Broadcom missed AI guidance and Nasdaq sold off the same day. Where exactly is this rotation going? Costco and Eli Lilly. That's defensive, not AI. $MSTR $BTC
tweetB:
:false,

tweetA.title: SpaceX IPO price SET — June 11-12 trading, $1.5T+ valuation, $75B raise = 3x largest US IPO ever
tweetA.idea: SpaceX IPO price set for June 11-12 trading at $1.5T+ valuation with a $75B raise — approximately 3x the largest US IPO in history. Arrives simultaneously with BTC liquidations, AI semi selloffs, pattern day trading rule removal. The $75B raise competes with every existing asset for the same pool of dollars.
tweetA.angle: $75B is not just an IPO — it's a market-structure event. Every dollar into SpaceX is a dollar not in NVDA, BTC, or anything else. Maximum capital demand, minimum fresh cash.
tweetA.tweetA: SpaceX IPO: June 11-12. $1.5T+ valuation. $75B raise — 3x the largest US IPO ever. This arrives while BTC is liquidating, AVGO just missed, and every risk asset competes for the same pool of capital. $75B is not a stock. It's a market structure event. $SPY $QQQ
tweetA.tweetB: $75B SpaceX raise while retail just got PDT rule removed, BTC is crashing, AVGO missed, and Saylor sold BTC. The supply of narratives is infinite. The supply of marginal dollars is not. Something has to give when the largest IPO ever competes with a leveraged stressed market.

tweetA.title: Samsung 45K-worker strike Day 10 of 18 — HBM/DRAM chokepoint at AI demand peak
tweetA.idea: Samsung's 45,000-worker strike on Day 10 of an 18-day planned action. Samsung is the world's largest memory chip producer and critical HBM and DRAM supplier. Labor disruption runs concurrently with AI demand peak for those chips. SK Hynix supply-constrained; Samsung both supply-constrained and labor-disrupted.
tweetA.angle: A Samsung strike at any other time is industrial news. At peak AI demand for HBM, it's a supply shock inside an existing supply shock. Q3 chip supply numbers will reflect this if it extends.
tweetA.tweetA: Samsung: 45,000 workers on strike, Day 10 of 18. The world's largest memory chip producer has a labor action at the exact moment HBM and DRAM demand is at a generational peak. SK Hynix is already sold out. Samsung disrupted = no backup supplier for AI memory. $005930 $NVDA
tweetA.tweetB: The Samsung strike is buried under AVGO and BTC news. But 45K workers at the world's largest memory producer on strike at AI demand peak is a supply-chain event that shows up in Q3 earnings. If it extends, HBM chokepoint becomes a crisis. Watch this quietly.

tweetA.title: SOL -15.6% on the week, DEX volume -82% — on-chain collapse worse than price suggests
tweetA.idea: Solana fell 15.6% on the week — worst among major cryptos. On-chain DEX volume collapsed 82%. When usage falls 82% while price falls 16%, the utility narrative is collapsing faster than the token price is pricing in.
tweetA.angle: Solana's bull thesis was always about activity: memecoins, DeFi, NFTs, trading volume. 82% DEX volume collapse means the thing that drove the narrative is gone. Price hasn't caught up yet.
tweetA.tweetA: SOL down 15.6% this week. But the real number: Solana DEX volume down 82%. Price is the lagging signal. When on-chain activity collapses 5x faster than price, the utility network narrative is dying faster than markets price. $SOL is playing catch-up to the downside.
tweetA.tweetB: Solana DEX volume -82% in a week. Not -16% like the token. The speculation infrastructure (memecoins, high-frequency trading, NFT mints) that drove the Solana thesis has essentially stopped. Price reflects the old narrative. The on-chain data reflects the new reality. $SOL

tweetA.title: Citi + Goldman: Nikkei 70K year-end target — Japan bull thesis intact despite -1.74% today
tweetA.idea: Citi and Goldman Sachs both bullish Japan medium-term, Nikkei 70,000 year-end target. Nikkei fell 1.74% today on global risk-off. Bull thesis: corporate governance reform, weak-yen boost to exporters, AI capex spillover. But the Yen carry trade that partially fuels Japan is the same trade that could unwind violently.
tweetA.angle: Nikkei at ~38-40K, target 70K — roughly 75-80% gain embedded in price targets. The Japan bull thesis is real but simultaneously runs on Yen carry that is a systemic risk if it unwinds.
tweetA.tweetA: Citi and Goldman targeting Nikkei 70K year-end. From current levels that's roughly 75-80% upside. Japan corporate reform + weak yen + AI capex spillover = real thesis. But the same Yen carry that supports that thesis is the mechanism that could unwind it catastrophically. $EWJ $DXJ
tweetA.tweetB: Nikkei 70K from two major banks. The Japan reform story is genuine. But the Yen carry trade is the hidden fuel, and Korea (same carry, more extreme bubble) is already cracking. Right on Japan fundamentals and still hurt by the carry unwind. $DXJ

tweetA.title: April PCE 3.8%, 10Y at 4.55%, gold $4,500 — the macro data into NFP morning
tweetA.idea: April PCE 3.8% YoY — 190bps above target. 10-year yield ~4.55%. Gold at $4,500.10 (+0.74%). Silver $73.65. The Fed is frozen, duration is painful, and gold is working as the only clean hedge.
tweetA.angle: Gold at $4,500 while everything else bleeds is the most important market signal of the week. Not just inflation-driven — it's fiscal risk and uncertainty premium expanding.
tweetA.tweetA: The scoreboard into NFP: PCE 3.8% (+190bps over target). 10Y at 4.55%. Gold $4,500. Everything bleeding except gold and defensives. Gold at $4,500 with 4.55% yields is not just an inflation hedge — it's a fiscal risk and uncertainty premium. That premium is expanding. $GLD
tweetA.tweetB: Gold $4,500. Silver $73.65. 10Y at 4.55%. PCE 3.8%. The one clean hedge is gold — not bonds (duration pain), not cash (losing to inflation), not crypto (capitulating). Gold at records with record yields tells you what sophisticated money thinks about the US fiscal path. $GLD $SLV

tweetA.title: McKinsey ConsumerWise: US consumers cutting discretionary spend — cautious ahead of summer
tweetA.idea: McKinsey ConsumerWise shows US consumers growing more cautious as rising cost concerns prompt plans to cut discretionary spending ahead of summer. The consumer who carried the economy through the initial tariff shock is now pulling back. Data center construction is propping aggregate demand metrics while underlying consumer behavior quietly shifts.
tweetA.angle: Consumer discretionary cuts ahead of summer is the retail sector's Q2 risk. Aggregate data will look resilient. The micro signal in consumer surveys is the lead indicator — typically 6-8 weeks ahead of earnings data.
tweetA.tweetA: McKinsey ConsumerWise: US consumers plan to cut discretionary spending ahead of summer. The consumer that carried the economy through tariff shock is now hesitating. The aggregate data looks fine because data center construction is propping it. The micro is telling a different story. $XRT $AMZN
tweetA.tweetB: Rising cost concerns → discretionary cuts. Not yet in retail earnings — it will be in Q2 reports. McKinsey consumer surveys lead earnings data by 6-8 weeks. Summer discretionary (travel, dining, entertainment) is where this shows up first. Watch July guidance calls. $XRT $LYFT

tweetA.title: Agentic shift in luxury — AI advisers shaping preference before customers enter stores
tweetA.idea: McKinsey research: agentic AI is pushing luxury purchasing decisions earlier in the customer journey. AI advisers providing personalized, adviser-like experiences at scale before the boutique visit. The brand that controls the AI-adviser layer controls the customer relationship — before the physical interaction.
